meaning: an approximate calculation
example:
Give me a rough estimate.
That's just a rough estimate.
A rough estimate would be £500.

meaning: the overall situation, not the details
example:
Try to see the big picture.
Don't lose sight of the big picture.
Focus on the big picture.

meaning: create a standard others are measured against
example:
Their service sets the benchmark.
This product sets the benchmark.
They set the benchmark for quality.

meaning: fixed and impossible to change
example:
The plan isn't set in stone.
Nothing is set in stone yet.
These rules are set in stone.

meaning: an advantage or control over others
example:
She gained the upper hand.
They have the upper hand now.
He fought to keep the upper hand.

meaning: something with both good and bad effects
example:
Fame is a double-edged sword.
Technology is a double-edged sword.
It's a double-edged sword.

meaning: a very difficult struggle
example:
It was an uphill battle to win.
Recovery is an uphill battle.
They face an uphill battle.

meaning: accept something as it appears
example:
Don't take ads at face value.
I took his words at face value.
Take the report at face value.

meaning: prepare the basis for future work
example:
They laid the groundwork for reform.
This lays the groundwork.
We laid the groundwork early.

meaning: a decision has not yet been made
example:
The jury is still out on the plan.
Is it healthy? The jury is still out.
The jury's still out on that.

meaning: a process where you must learn quickly
example:
The new job has a steep learning curve.
It was a steep learning curve.
Expect a steep learning curve.

meaning: unfairly change the rules or aims
example:
They keep moving the goalposts.
Stop moving the goalposts.
The boss moved the goalposts again.

meaning: not to be made public
example:
This is strictly off the record.
He spoke off the record.
Off the record, I disagree.

meaning: decide as you go, without a plan
example:
Let's play it by ear.
We'll play it by ear.
I prefer to play it by ear.

meaning: excessive official rules and paperwork
example:
There's too much red tape.
Red tape slowed the project.
We got stuck in red tape.

meaning: all day and all night
example:
They worked around the clock.
The line is open around the clock.
Staff are on duty around the clock.

meaning: take control or charge
example:
She took the reins of the company.
It's time to take the reins.
He took the reins after she left.

meaning: fully informed or at the required level
example:
Let me get you up to speed.
I'm not up to speed yet.
Bring the team up to speed.

meaning: an outcome good for everyone
example:
It's a win-win situation.
This deal is win-win.
We found a win-win solution.

meaning: start something energetically and effectively
example:
She hit the ground running on day one.
We need to hit the ground running.
He hit the ground running in the role.
